mnemonics: FINITE: Few Items, Numerable, In Total, Ending. INFINITE: Immeasurably Numerous, Forever Increasing, Never-ending, Impossibly To Enumerate.
keyPoints: A set is finite if its elements can be put into a one-to-one correspondence with the set {1, 2, ..., n} for some natural number n.
A set is infinite if it is not finite. This means no such natural number n exists to count its elements.
In the context of metric spaces, the properties of sets (like being open/closed or compact) often depend on whether they are finite or infinite.
commonMistakes: Confusing a bounded set with a finite set. An infinite set (like the interval [0,1]) can be bounded within a metric space.
Assuming a set with an infinite number of elements must be uncountable. Many important infinite sets in analysis (like the set of natural numbers ℕ or rational numbers ℚ) are countably infinite.
